questions.prompt: The lower cutoff frequency of an amplifier is caused by:
options: Coupling and bypass capacitors acting as high-pass filters
questions.correctAnswer: Coupling and bypass capacitors acting as high-pass filters
questions.explanation: Coupling capacitors block DC but pass AC. At low frequencies, their impedance (1/ωC) becomes large, creating a voltage divider that attenuates the signal. This creates a high-pass filter with a lower cutoff frequency.

questions.prompt: The lower cutoff frequency for a coupling capacitor C_c with input resistance R_in is:
questions.explanation: The RC high-pass filter formed by C_c and R_in has cutoff frequency ω_L = 1/(R_inC_c), or f_L = 1/(2πR_inC_c). Below this frequency, the gain drops at 20 dB/decade.

questions.prompt: At the -3 dB cutoff frequency, the voltage is what fraction of its midband value?
questions.explanation: -3 dB corresponds to half power. Since power ∝ V², half power means V²/2, so V = V_max/√2 ≈ 0.707 × V_max. This is the standard definition of cutoff frequency.

questions.prompt: If three capacitors produce individual cutoffs at 10 Hz, 30 Hz, and 5 Hz, the amplifier's lower cutoff is:
questions.explanation: The amplifier's actual lower cutoff is the HIGHEST of all individual cutoffs. The 30 Hz capacitor starts attenuating first as frequency decreases, creating the limiting bottleneck for the amplifier.

questions.prompt: The upper cutoff frequency is caused by:
options: Coupling capacitors having too high impedance
Parasitic capacitances (C_be, C_bc) shunting signal current at high frequencies
The power supply running out of current
questions.correctAnswer: Parasitic capacitances (C_be, C_bc) shunting signal current at high frequencies
questions.explanation: At high frequencies, the tiny parasitic capacitances (picofarads) between BJT terminals have low impedance (X_C = 1/ωC), creating low-impedance paths that bypass the normal amplifier action and reduce gain.

questions.prompt: If R_in = 10 kΩ and you want f_L < 20 Hz, the minimum coupling capacitor is:
questions.explanation: C > 1/(2πf_L R_in) = 1/(2π × 20 × 10⁴) = 1/(1.257×10⁵) = 7.96×10⁻⁶ F ≈ 0.796 μF. In practice, you'd choose at least 1 μF to have margin.

questions.prompt: The 'midband' region of an amplifier is where:
options: The gain is at its maximum and approximately constant
questions.correctAnswer: The gain is at its maximum and approximately constant
questions.explanation: In the midband (f_L < f < f_H), coupling capacitors effectively short-circuit (low impedance) and parasitic capacitors effectively open-circuit (high impedance). The gain is at its flat maximum — this is where all our small-signal analysis applies.
BW = f_H - f_L is the frequency range over which the amplifier provides useful gain (within 3 dB of the midband value). Since typically f_H >> f_L, BW ≈ f_H.

questions.prompt: Why do we use large coupling capacitors (e.g., 100 μF)?
options: To make f_L as low as possible so all audio frequencies pass
To store energy for the bias circuit
questions.correctAnswer: To make f_L as low as possible so all audio frequencies pass
questions.explanation: f_L = 1/(2πRC). Larger C means smaller f_L. With C = 100 μF and R = 10 kΩ, f_L = 0.16 Hz — well below the 20 Hz lower limit of human hearing, ensuring all audio frequencies pass without attenuation.

questions.prompt: The emitter bypass capacitor affects bandwidth because:
options: At low frequencies it fails to short R_E2, increasing emitter resistance and reducing gain
It increases the upper cutoff frequency
It has no effect on bandwidth
questions.correctAnswer: At low frequencies it fails to short R_E2, increasing emitter resistance and reducing gain
questions.explanation: The bypass capacitor shorts R_E2 at midband frequencies, increasing gain. At low frequencies, C_E has high impedance and no longer bypasses R_E2, so the full R_E appears in the circuit, reducing gain. This creates a cutoff frequency.
